screens.prompt: The SCF is a theoretical quantity defined from an infinite-time average. You only ever have a finite, noisy recording of the burst. Do you expect a raw, finite-data estimate of the SCF to suffer from the same kind of problem the raw periodogram had for the ordinary PSD?
choices: No — the extra cycle-frequency dimension somehow avoids the periodogram's variance problem
Yes — a raw 'cyclic periodogram' from finite data is just as inconsistent an estimator as the ordinary periodogram was
screens.reveal: Yes — the same disease, in a bigger space. A raw cyclic periodogram computed from one finite recording is every bit as inconsistent as the ordinary periodogram: its variance doesn't shrink just because the recording is long. The fix is the same idea generalized to two dimensions: **time-smoothing** (averaging over shifted short-time cyclic periodograms — the direct 2D cousin of Bartlett/Welch segment-averaging) and **frequency-smoothing** (averaging over nearby frequency bins in f). Both trade resolution — now in *two* dimensions, f and α — for reduced variance.

screens.prompt: Time-smoothing cyclic spectral estimators are the direct two-dimensional analogue of which technique from the ordinary spectral-estimation module?

choices.text: Welch's method — averaging periodograms of shifted/overlapping segments

choices.feedback: Zero-padding only interpolates an existing spectrum's samples more finely — it doesn't reduce variance at all, which is the actual problem time-smoothing solves.
Sample rate sets the Nyquist bandwidth you can observe, not the variance of the spectral estimate — a higher sample rate alone doesn't fix the periodogram's inconsistency.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — time-smoothing generalizes Welch/Bartlett segment-averaging into the (f, α) plane, and frequency-smoothing generalizes windowed/smoothed periodogram estimation the same way. Same statistical fix, one more axis to apply it over.
